1:
"Likewise, ye wives, be in subjection to your own husbands; that, if any obey not the word, they also may without the word be won by the conversation of the wives;" The witnesses note the call to wives to win unbelieving husbands by their conduct. On "ye wives, be in subjection to your own husbands," the grammatical voices note the duty. Barnes and Robertson note the exhortation continuing the theme of submission (2:13ff.): "ye wives, be in subjection (submit yourselves) to your own husbands" — Christian wives are to submit to their husbands, in the ordered relation of the household; "likewise" links it to the general duty of submission.

On "if any obey not the word," the witnesses note the unbelieving husband. Barnes and Calvin note the special case: "if any obey not the word (are unbelievers, do not accept the gospel)" — some wives had husbands who were not Christians; the counsel addresses how such a wife should behave toward an unbelieving husband.

On "may without the word be won by the conversation of the wives," the puritan Henry notes the silent witness: such husbands "may without the word (without preaching, without argument) be won (gained to the faith) by the conversation (manner of life, conduct) of the wives" — the wife's holy, submissive behaviour may win the husband to Christ more powerfully than words; a godly life preaches where speech cannot. The verse displays the call to wives to win unbelieving husbands by their conduct.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the duty — "ye wives, be in subjection to your own husbands," wives submitting in the ordered household, continuing the theme of submission (Barnes, Robertson); "if any obey not the word," the case of an unbelieving husband (Barnes, Calvin); "without the word be won by the conversation (conduct) of the wives," the wife's holy life winning the husband without argument (Henry). The verse displays the winning conduct. The verse marks the wives: in subjection. The verse displays the husbands: some unbelieving. The verse displays the means: without the word. The verse displays the power: holy conduct. The counsel displays the wife winning the husband by her life. The verse displays the subjection: enjoined. The verse displays the word: unobeyed. The verse displays the conversation: winning. The call to wives to win unbelieving husbands by their conduct displays the wisdom of the exhortation: Christian wives, by submitting and by their holy manner of life, may win even husbands who reject the gospel — without a word, the conduct of the wives doing what argument cannot. The verse displays the winning life, marked by chastity and fear (v.2).
2:
"While they behold your chaste conversation coupled with fear." The brief verse notes the chaste conduct joined with fear that wins the husband. The grammatical voices note the means of winning. Barnes and Robertson note how the husband is won: "while they behold (observe, watch) your chaste (pure) conversation (conduct, manner of life) coupled with fear (reverence)" — the unbelieving husband, watching his wife's pure and reverent behaviour day by day, is moved by what he sees.

On "chaste conversation," the witnesses note the purity. Barnes and Calvin note that "chaste conversation" is a pure, modest, holy manner of life — blameless conduct that adorns the gospel; the wife's purity is the silent argument.

On "coupled with fear," the grammatical-historical note the reverence: "with fear" — either reverence toward God, or respectful regard toward the husband; a holy fear that keeps the conduct pure and modest; the chaste life and the reverent spirit together win the husband. The verse displays the chaste conduct joined with fear that wins the husband.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the means — "while they behold (observe) your chaste (pure) conversation (conduct) coupled with fear (reverence)," the husband moved by watching his wife's pure, reverent behaviour (Barnes, Robertson); "chaste conversation" a pure, modest, holy life, the silent argument (Barnes, Calvin); "with fear," reverence toward God or respect toward the husband, keeping the conduct pure (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the chaste life. The verse marks the beholding: by the husband. The verse displays the conduct: chaste. The verse displays the fear: reverent. The verse displays the effect: winning. The conduct displays the pure, reverent life that wins. The verse displays the chastity: observed. The verse displays the fear: coupled. The verse displays the conversation: holy. The chaste conduct joined with fear that wins the husband displays how the silent witness works: as the unbelieving husband beholds his wife's pure and reverent manner of life, that holy conduct moves him; the chaste life coupled with godly fear is the wife's winning argument. The verse displays the winning chastity, over against mere outward adorning (v.3).
3:
"Whose adorning let it not be that outward adorning of plaiting the hair, and of wearing of gold, or of putting on of apparel;" The witnesses note the warning against mere outward adornment. On "let it not be that outward adorning," the grammatical voices note the contrast being set up. Barnes and Robertson note the counsel on adornment: "whose adorning (kosmos — ornamentation) let it not be that outward adorning of plaiting (braiding, elaborate arrangement) the hair, and of wearing of gold (gold jewelry), or of putting on of apparel (fine clothing)" — the wife's true adornment is not to consist in elaborate hairstyles, gold ornaments, and fine clothes.

On "outward adorning," the witnesses note the relative prohibition. Barnes and Calvin note that the apostle does not absolutely forbid all clothing or ornament (which would be absurd) but warns against making outward show the chief adornment; the emphasis falls on "let it not be" the principal thing — excessive, vain external display is what is checked.

On the principle, the puritan Henry and wesleyan-arminian Wesley note the heart: true beauty is not in external finery but in the inward graces (v.4); the warning is against vanity and worldliness in dress, which betray a wrong estimate of what is precious. The verse displays the warning against mere outward adornment.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the counsel — "let it not be that outward adorning of plaiting the hair, and of wearing of gold, or of putting on of apparel," the true adornment not elaborate hair, gold, and fine clothes (Barnes, Robertson); not an absolute prohibition of all ornament but against making outward show the chief thing, excessive vain display checked (Barnes, Calvin); the warning against vanity in dress, true beauty inward (Henry, Wesley). The verse displays the outward adorning. The verse marks the adorning: not outward. The verse displays the hair: plaited. The verse displays the gold: worn. The verse displays the apparel: fine. The counsel displays external display not to be the chief adornment. The verse displays the outward: checked. The verse displays the gold: not the beauty. The verse displays the apparel: not the glory. The warning against mere outward adornment displays the counsel on the wife's true beauty: her adornment is not to consist in braided hair, gold jewelry, and costly clothing — not that all ornament is forbidden, but that vain outward display must not be her glory. The verse displays the outward adorning, surpassed by the hidden ornament of the heart (v.4).
4:
"But let it be the hidden man of the heart, in that which is not corruptible, even the ornament of a meek and quiet spirit, which is in the sight of God of great price." The witnesses note the true adornment: the meek and quiet spirit, precious to God. On "the hidden man of the heart," the grammatical voices note the inward person. Barnes and Robertson note the contrast with outward show: "let it be the hidden (inner, concealed) man of the heart (the inward person, the character of the soul)" — the true adornment is the inward self, the hidden character, not the outward appearance.

On "in that which is not corruptible, even the ornament of a meek and quiet spirit," the witnesses note the imperishable beauty. Barnes and Calvin note the contrast of the perishable and imperishable: "in that which is not corruptible (incorruptible, enduring), even the ornament of a meek (gentle) and quiet (tranquil, peaceable) spirit" — unlike fading external finery, the meek and quiet spirit is an incorruptible adornment; gentleness and tranquility of soul are the true and lasting beauty.

On "in the sight of God of great price," the eastern Cyril notes God's valuation: this ornament is "in the sight of God of great price (very costly, precious)" — God esteems the meek and quiet spirit as of great worth; what the world counts plain, God counts precious; his estimate, not the world's, defines true beauty. The verse displays the true adornment: the meek and quiet spirit, precious to God.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the inward person — "the hidden (inner) man of the heart," the true adornment the inward self, not the outward (Barnes, Robertson); "not corruptible, even the ornament of a meek (gentle) and quiet spirit," the incorruptible adornment of a gentle, tranquil soul, unlike fading finery (Barnes, Calvin); "in the sight of God of great price," God esteeming it precious, his estimate defining true beauty (Cyril). The verse displays the inward beauty. The verse marks the adornment: the hidden man. The verse displays the spirit: meek and quiet. The verse displays the quality: incorruptible. The verse displays the worth: precious to God. The adornment displays the gentle soul prized by God. The verse displays the heart: adorned. The verse displays the spirit: meek. The verse displays the price: great with God. The true adornment — the meek and quiet spirit, precious to God — displays the beauty that surpasses all outward finery: not fading hair and gold, but the hidden person of the heart, the incorruptible ornament of a gentle and tranquil spirit, which God himself esteems as of great price. The verse displays the precious spirit, exemplified by the holy women of old (vv.5-6).
5:
"For after this manner in the old time the holy women also, who trusted in God, adorned themselves, being in subjection unto their own husbands:" The brief verse notes the holy women of old adorned with submission and trust. The grammatical voices note the example. Barnes and Robertson note the appeal to precedent: "after this manner in the old time the holy women (the godly women of the Old Testament) also, who trusted (hoped) in God, adorned themselves" — the saintly women of old adorned themselves in this inward way, with the meek spirit, not outward finery.

On "who trusted in God," the witnesses note their faith. Barnes and Calvin note that these women "trusted in God" — their adornment sprang from faith; their hope was in God, not in outward show; the inward beauty was the fruit of their trust.

On "being in subjection unto their own husbands," the puritan Henry notes the submission: their adorning included "being in subjection unto their own husbands" — the holy women's true ornament was their submissive, reverent spirit toward their husbands; submission was part of their inward beauty; the example sets the pattern for Christian wives. The verse displays the holy women of old adorned with submission and trust.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the example — "the holy women (of old) also, who trusted in God, adorned themselves" in this inward way, with the meek spirit (Barnes, Robertson); "who trusted in God," their adornment springing from faith, hope in God not show (Barnes, Calvin); "being in subjection unto their own husbands," submission part of their inward beauty, the pattern for wives (Henry). The verse displays the holy women. The verse marks the women: holy, of old. The verse displays the trust: in God. The verse displays the adorning: inward. The verse displays the subjection: to husbands. The example displays godly women adorned with faith and submission. The verse displays the women: trusting. The verse displays the adorning: meek. The verse displays the subjection: practiced. The holy women of old adorned with submission and trust display the example that grounds the counsel: the godly women of former times, who hoped in God, adorned themselves with the inward beauty of a meek spirit and submission to their husbands. The verse displays the pattern of the holy women, supremely Sarah (v.6).
6:
"Even as Sara obeyed Abraham, calling him lord: whose daughters ye are, as long as ye do well, and are not afraid with any amazement." The witnesses note Sarah's example, whose daughters the faithful wives are. On "even as Sara obeyed Abraham, calling him lord," the grammatical voices note the chief example. Barnes and Robertson note the model of submission: "even as Sara obeyed (hypekouen — submitted to) Abraham, calling him lord (a title of respect, Genesis 18:12)" — Sarah is the supreme example, who obeyed her husband and called him "lord," showing her respectful submission.

On "whose daughters ye are," the witnesses note the spiritual descent. Barnes and Calvin note that Christian wives are "whose daughters ye are (Sarah's daughters)" — they become the true daughters of Sarah, her spiritual offspring, by imitating her faith and submission; as believers are sons of Abraham by faith, so godly wives are daughters of Sarah.

On "as long as ye do well, and are not afraid with any amazement," the puritan Henry notes the condition: they are Sarah's daughters "as long as ye do well (do what is right), and are not afraid with any amazement (any terror, not driven by fear)" — the condition is doing good and not being overcome by fear; acting from faith, not from terror; living uprightly without being frightened from their duty. The verse displays Sarah's example, whose daughters the faithful wives are.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the model — "even as Sara obeyed Abraham, calling him lord," Sarah the supreme example of respectful submission (Genesis 18:12, Barnes, Robertson); "whose daughters ye are," Christian wives the spiritual daughters of Sarah by imitating her faith and submission (Barnes, Calvin); "as long as ye do well, and are not afraid with any amazement," the condition doing good and not being driven by fear (Henry). The verse displays Sarah's daughters. The verse marks the example: Sarah. The verse displays the obedience: to Abraham. The verse displays the descent: her daughters. The verse displays the condition: do well, fear not. The example displays godly wives as Sarah's daughters by faith. The verse displays the Sarah: obeying. The verse displays the daughters: faithful. The verse displays the fear: overcome. Sarah's example, whose daughters the faithful wives are, displays the crown of the appeal: as Sarah obeyed Abraham and called him lord, so Christian wives become her true daughters by imitating her — so long as they do well and are not driven from their duty by any fear. The verse displays Sarah's daughters, balanced by the husband's duty (v.7).
7:
"Likewise, ye husbands, dwell with them according to knowledge, giving honour unto the wife, as unto the weaker vessel, and as being heirs together of the grace of life; that your prayers be not hindered." The witnesses note the husband's duty: to dwell with the wife in honour as a joint heir. On "ye husbands, dwell with them according to knowledge," the grammatical voices note the husband's wisdom. Barnes and Robertson note the reciprocal duty: "ye husbands, dwell with them according to knowledge (with understanding, considerately, wisely)" — the husband is to live with his wife thoughtfully, with understanding of her nature and needs; the duty is mutual, not submission only on the wife's part.

On "giving honour unto the wife, as unto the weaker vessel," the witnesses note the honour due. Barnes and Calvin note: "giving honour (respect, esteem) unto the wife, as unto the weaker (more delicate, physically frailer) vessel" — the husband is to honour his wife, and her being the "weaker vessel" is a reason for honour and tender care, not contempt; weakness calls for protection and respect.

On "heirs together of the grace of life; that your prayers be not hindered," the puritan Henry notes the spiritual equality and the warning: husband and wife are "heirs together of the grace of life (joint heirs of eternal life)" — equal before God in grace; and the husband must so live "that your prayers be not hindered (interrupted, obstructed)" — marital discord or failure in duty hinders prayer; harmony is necessary for unhindered fellowship with God. The verse displays the husband's duty to dwell with the wife in honour as a joint heir.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the husband's wisdom — "dwell with them according to knowledge (considerately)," living thoughtfully with understanding of the wife (Barnes, Robertson); "giving honour... as unto the weaker vessel," honouring the wife, her weakness a reason for care not contempt (Barnes, Calvin); "heirs together of the grace of life; that your prayers be not hindered," joint heirs of eternal life, discord hindering prayer (Henry). The verse displays the husband's duty. The verse marks the dwelling: with knowledge. The verse displays the honour: to the wife. The verse displays the weakness: a call for care. The verse displays the inheritance: joint heirs. The duty displays the husband honouring the wife as a fellow heir. The verse displays the husband: considerate. The verse displays the honour: given. The verse displays the prayers: unhindered. The husband's duty to dwell with the wife in honour as a joint heir displays the reciprocal obligation: the husband is to live considerately with his wife, honouring her as the weaker vessel and as a fellow heir of eternal life — lest marital discord hinder his prayers. The verse displays the husband's honour, opening the call to general love and unity (v.8).
8:
"Finally, be ye all of one mind, having compassion one of another, love as brethren, be pitiful, be courteous:" The witnesses note the call to unity, compassion, brotherly love, pity, and courtesy. On "be ye all of one mind," the grammatical voices note the unity. Barnes and Robertson note the summary exhortation to all: "finally, be ye all of one mind (homophrones — harmonious, of one accord, like-minded)" — all believers, not wives and husbands only, are to be united in mind and purpose; harmony in the church.

On "having compassion one of another, love as brethren," the witnesses note sympathy and love. Barnes and Calvin note the graces: "having compassion (sympatheis — sympathizing, feeling with) one of another, love as brethren (philadelphoi — loving as a family)" — believers are to share one another's joys and sorrows, and to love one another with the affection of brothers; mutual sympathy and family love.

On "be pitiful, be courteous," the puritan Henry notes the remaining graces: "be pitiful (eusplanchnoi — tenderhearted, compassionate), be courteous (philophrones — friendly, kind, humble-minded)" — tenderhearted compassion and gracious, kindly courtesy; the cluster of social graces that should mark the Christian community. The verse displays the call to unity, compassion, brotherly love, pity, and courtesy.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the unity — "be ye all of one mind (harmonious, like-minded)," all believers united in mind (Barnes, Robertson); "having compassion (sympathizing) one of another, love as brethren," mutual sympathy and family love (Barnes, Calvin); "be pitiful (tenderhearted), be courteous (kind, humble)," tenderhearted compassion and gracious courtesy (Henry). The verse displays the social graces. The verse marks the mind: one. The verse displays the compassion: mutual. The verse displays the love: as brethren. The verse displays the graces: pity, courtesy. The call displays the harmonious, loving, kindly community. The verse displays the unity: enjoined. The verse displays the compassion: shared. The verse displays the courtesy: shown. The call to unity, compassion, brotherly love, pity, and courtesy displays the cluster of graces for the Christian community: all are to be of one mind, sympathizing with one another, loving as a family, tenderhearted and courteous. The verse displays the graces of community, which return good for evil (v.9).
9:
"Not rendering evil for evil, or railing for railing: but contrariwise blessing; knowing that ye are thereunto called, that ye should inherit a blessing." The witnesses note the call to return blessing for evil, that we may inherit blessing. On "not rendering evil for evil, or railing for railing," the grammatical voices note the prohibition of retaliation. Barnes and Robertson note the command: "not rendering (paying back, returning) evil for evil, or railing (loidorian — abuse, reviling) for railing" — the believer is not to retaliate, not to repay injury with injury or insult with insult; the renunciation of revenge.

On "but contrariwise blessing," the witnesses note the positive duty. Barnes and Calvin note the opposite course: "but contrariwise (on the contrary) blessing" — instead of returning evil, the believer is to bless those who wrong him; to return good for evil, blessing for cursing (cf. Matthew 5:44; Romans 12:17).

On "knowing that ye are thereunto called, that ye should inherit a blessing," the puritan Henry notes the motive: "ye are thereunto called (to this very thing), that ye should inherit a blessing" — believers are called to bless precisely so that they may inherit a blessing; God's calling and the promised inheritance are both bound up with the blessing spirit; those who bless are blessed. The verse displays the call to return blessing for evil, that we may inherit blessing.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the prohibition — "not rendering (returning) evil for evil, or railing (reviling) for railing," not retaliating with injury or insult (Barnes, Robertson); "but contrariwise blessing," returning good for evil, blessing for cursing (Matthew 5:44, Barnes, Calvin); "thereunto called, that ye should inherit a blessing," called to bless so as to inherit blessing, those who bless are blessed (Henry). The verse displays the blessing returned. The verse marks the evil: not returned. The verse displays the railing: not repaid. The verse displays the response: blessing. The verse displays the motive: inherit a blessing. The call displays good returned for evil, that blessing be inherited. The verse displays the evil: renounced. The verse displays the blessing: given. The verse displays the inheritance: a blessing. The call to return blessing for evil, that we may inherit blessing, displays the renunciation of revenge: the believer is not to repay evil with evil or insult with insult, but to bless instead — for to this we are called, that we may inherit a blessing. The verse displays the blessing spirit, grounded in the Psalm of the good life (vv.10-12).
10:
"For he that will love life, and see good days, let him refrain his tongue from evil, and his lips that they speak no guile:" The brief verse notes the counsel from the Psalm: guard the tongue to love life. The grammatical voices note the citation. Barnes and Robertson note the quotation (Psalm 34:12-16): "he that will love life, and see good days (he who desires to enjoy life and see good days), let him refrain (restrain, keep) his tongue from evil, and his lips that they speak no guile (deceit)" — Peter cites the Psalm to ground the call to bless: the one who would love life must control his speech, keeping it free from evil and deceit.

On "refrain his tongue from evil," the witnesses note speech. Barnes and Calvin note that the first requirement of the good life is to guard the tongue — to keep it from evil words, slander, and reviling; right speech is foundational to the blessed life.

On "lips that they speak no guile," the grammatical-historical note sincerity: "no guile (deceit, treachery)" — the lips must be free not only of open evil but of deceit; sincerity and truthfulness mark the speech of the one who would see good days. The verse displays the counsel from the Psalm: guard the tongue to love life.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the citation (Psalm 34) — "he that will love life, and see good days, let him refrain his tongue from evil, and his lips that they speak no guile," the one who would enjoy life controlling his speech (Barnes, Robertson); guarding the tongue from evil words and reviling, foundational to the good life (Barnes, Calvin); "no guile," lips free of deceit, sincerity marking the speech (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the guarded tongue. The verse marks the desire: love life. The verse displays the tongue: refrained from evil. The verse displays the lips: no guile. The verse displays the requirement: right speech. The counsel displays the good life requiring controlled, sincere speech. The verse displays the life: loved. The verse displays the tongue: guarded. The verse displays the guile: avoided. The counsel from the Psalm — guard the tongue to love life — displays the scriptural ground of the blessing spirit: whoever would love life and see good days must restrain his tongue from evil and keep his lips from deceit. The verse displays the guarded speech, joined to doing good and seeking peace (v.11).
11:
"Let him eschew evil, and do good; let him seek peace, and ensue it." The brief verse notes the counsel to turn from evil, do good, and pursue peace. The grammatical voices note the continued citation. Barnes and Robertson note the Psalm's further requirements: "let him eschew (turn away from, shun) evil, and do good; let him seek peace, and ensue it (pursue it, follow after it)" — beyond guarded speech, the good life requires turning from evil, doing good, and actively pursuing peace.

On "eschew evil, and do good," the witnesses note the negative and positive. Barnes and Calvin note the twofold duty: not merely avoiding evil but actively doing good; the blessed life is both departure from evil and pursuit of good.

On "seek peace, and ensue it," the grammatical-historical note the eager pursuit: "seek peace, and ensue (pursue) it" — peace is not merely to be desired but actively sought and pursued, as one chases what flees; the believer is to be a diligent peacemaker. The verse displays the counsel to turn from evil, do good, and pursue peace.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the requirements — "let him eschew (shun) evil, and do good; let him seek peace, and ensue (pursue) it," turning from evil, doing good, pursuing peace (Barnes, Robertson); not merely avoiding evil but actively doing good (Barnes, Calvin); "seek peace, and ensue it," peace actively pursued as one chases what flees (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the good life. The verse marks the evil: eschewed. The verse displays the good: done. The verse displays the peace: sought. The verse displays the pursuit: eager. The counsel displays turning from evil and pursuing good and peace. The verse displays the evil: shunned. The verse displays the good: practiced. The verse displays the peace: chased. The counsel to turn from evil, do good, and pursue peace displays the further requirements of the blessed life: the one who would love life must shun evil, actively do good, and diligently seek and pursue peace. The verse displays the pursuit of peace, watched over by the Lord's eyes (v.12).
12:
"For the eyes of the Lord are over the righteous, and his ears are open unto their prayers: but the face of the Lord is against them that do evil." The witnesses note the Lord's care for the righteous and his opposition to evildoers. On "the eyes of the Lord are over the righteous," the grammatical voices note the divine regard. Barnes and Robertson note the Psalm's ground (Psalm 34): "the eyes of the Lord are over (upon) the righteous" — God watches over the righteous with favour and care; his providential eye is on them for good.

On "his ears are open unto their prayers," the witnesses note answered prayer. Barnes and Calvin note that "his ears are open unto their prayers" — God hears and answers the prayers of the righteous; the blessed life of the godly enjoys access to God and the assurance of being heard.

On "the face of the Lord is against them that do evil," the eastern Cyril and grammatical-historical note the warning: "the face of the Lord is against (set against, opposed to) them that do evil" — God's countenance is turned against evildoers in displeasure and judgment; the same God who favours the righteous opposes the wicked; the motive both to do good and to fear doing evil. The verse displays the Lord's care for the righteous and his opposition to evildoers.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the divine regard — "the eyes of the Lord are over (upon) the righteous," God watching over the righteous with favour (Barnes, Robertson); "his ears are open unto their prayers," God hearing and answering the godly (Barnes, Calvin); "the face of the Lord is against them that do evil," God's countenance set against evildoers in judgment (Cyril, grammatical-historical). The verse displays God's regard. The verse marks the eyes: over the righteous. The verse displays the ears: open to prayer. The verse displays the face: against evildoers. The verse displays the motive: favour and fear. The Psalm displays God favouring the righteous and opposing the wicked. The verse displays the righteous: watched. The verse displays the prayers: heard. The verse displays the evil: opposed. The Lord's care for the righteous and his opposition to evildoers display the ground of the whole counsel: God's eyes are over the righteous and his ears open to their prayers, but his face is against those who do evil — a double motive to pursue good and shun evil. The verse displays the watchful Lord, who guards those zealous for good (v.13).
13:
"And who is he that will harm you, if ye be followers of that which is good?" The brief verse notes the question: who will harm those zealous for good? The grammatical voices note the rhetorical assurance. Barnes and Robertson note the encouraging question: "who is he that will harm (injure, do real evil to) you, if ye be followers (zealous imitators, eager pursuers) of that which is good?" — generally, those who are devoted to doing good are protected; few will wish to harm, and none can truly harm, the one zealous for good.

On "followers of that which is good," the witnesses note the zeal. Barnes and Calvin note that "followers (zealots, eager pursuers) of that which is good" means those passionately devoted to good; their goodness is itself a protection, disarming enemies and winning favour.

On "who is he that will harm you," the grammatical-historical note the qualified sense: the general rule is that goodness protects, though (as v.14 acknowledges) the righteous may still suffer; the deeper meaning is that no real, ultimate harm can come to the one zealous for good, for God watches over him (v.12). The verse displays the question: who will harm those zealous for good?

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the assurance — "who is he that will harm (injure) you, if ye be followers (zealous pursuers) of that which is good?" those devoted to good generally protected, none truly harming them (Barnes, Robertson); "followers of that which is good," passionate devotion to good itself a protection (Barnes, Calvin); the general rule that goodness protects, no real harm coming to the godly whom God watches (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the protecting good. The verse marks the question: who will harm. The verse displays the followers: of good. The verse displays the zeal: eager. The verse displays the assurance: protection. The question displays goodness shielding its zealous followers. The verse displays the harm: averted. The verse displays the good: pursued. The verse displays the safety: in goodness. The question — who will harm those zealous for good? — displays the assurance that follows the Psalm: as a general rule, those eagerly devoted to good are safe, for few will wish and none can truly harm the one whom God watches over. The verse displays the protected good, even where suffering comes for righteousness (v.14).
14:
"But and if ye suffer for righteousness' sake, happy are ye: and be not afraid of their terror, neither be troubled;" The witnesses note that suffering for righteousness is blessedness, free from fear. On "if ye suffer for righteousness' sake, happy are ye," the grammatical voices note the beatitude. Barnes and Robertson note the echo of Christ's words (Matthew 5:10): "if ye suffer for righteousness' sake (on account of doing right), happy (blessed) are ye" — even when the godly do suffer for doing right, they are blessed; suffering for righteousness is no calamity but a blessedness.

On "be not afraid of their terror," the witnesses note the freedom from fear. Barnes and Calvin note the citation (Isaiah 8:12-13): "be not afraid of their terror (do not fear what they fear, or do not be frightened by their threats)" — the believer is not to be terrified by the threats or the dread that persecutors inspire; the fear of man is to be cast off.

On "neither be troubled," the puritan Henry notes the inward calm: "neither be troubled (agitated, disturbed in heart)" — the believer's heart is to remain untroubled, at peace, even amid persecution; the antidote follows (v.15) — sanctifying the Lord in the heart. The verse displays that suffering for righteousness is blessedness, free from fear.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the beatitude — "if ye suffer for righteousness' sake, happy (blessed) are ye," suffering for doing right a blessedness, not calamity (Matthew 5:10, Barnes, Robertson); "be not afraid of their terror" (Isaiah 8:12), not frightened by persecutors' threats, the fear of man cast off (Barnes, Calvin); "neither be troubled," the heart untroubled, at peace amid persecution (Henry). The verse displays the blessed sufferer. The verse marks the suffering: for righteousness. The verse displays the state: happy. The verse displays the fear: cast off. The verse displays the heart: untroubled. The beatitude displays suffering for right as blessedness, free from fear. The verse displays the righteousness: suffered for. The verse displays the terror: not feared. The verse displays the trouble: stilled. That suffering for righteousness is blessedness, free from fear, displays the encouragement for persecuted believers: even if they suffer for doing right, they are blessed; they need not fear their persecutors' threats nor be troubled in heart. The verse displays the fearless sufferer, who sanctifies the Lord in the heart (v.15).
15:
"But sanctify the Lord God in your hearts: and be ready always to give an answer to every man that asketh you a reason of the hope that is in you with meekness and fear:" The witnesses note the call to sanctify the Lord and be ready to give a reason for hope. On "sanctify the Lord God in your hearts," the grammatical voices note the inward reverence. Barnes and Robertson note the antidote to fear (the many texts read "sanctify Christ as Lord"): "sanctify the Lord God (set apart, hallow, reverence the Lord, or Christ as Lord) in your hearts" — to sanctify the Lord in the heart is to give him the supreme place, to reverence and trust him above all; this inward enthronement of God casts out the fear of man.

On "be ready always to give an answer to every man that asketh you a reason of the hope," the witnesses note the readiness to witness. Barnes and Calvin note the duty: "be ready always to give an answer (apologian — a defense, reasoned account) to every man that asketh you a reason (logon — an account) of the hope that is in you" — the believer must always be prepared to explain and defend the Christian hope when asked; an intelligent, ready account of one's faith.

On "with meekness and fear," the puritan Henry notes the manner: the defense is to be made "with meekness (gentleness, not arrogance or contention) and fear (reverence, humility, godly seriousness)" — the witness is given humbly and reverently, not harshly; the spirit of the answer matters as much as its substance. The verse displays the call to sanctify the Lord and be ready to give a reason for hope.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the inward reverence — "sanctify the Lord God (or Christ as Lord) in your hearts," hallowing and trusting the Lord supremely, casting out the fear of man (Barnes, Robertson); "be ready always to give an answer (defense)... of the hope," always prepared to explain and defend the Christian hope (Barnes, Calvin); "with meekness and fear," the defense given gently and reverently, the spirit mattering (Henry). The verse displays the ready witness. The verse marks the sanctifying: the Lord in the heart. The verse displays the readiness: always. The verse displays the answer: a reason for hope. The verse displays the manner: meekness and fear. The call displays the Lord enthroned within and the hope defended humbly. The verse displays the Lord: sanctified. The verse displays the hope: explained. The verse displays the meekness: maintained. The call to sanctify the Lord and be ready to give a reason for hope displays the believer's answer to persecution: enthrone the Lord in the heart (which casts out fear), and be always ready to give a reasoned, humble defense of the Christian hope to any who ask — with meekness and reverence. The verse displays the ready, gentle witness, guarded by a good conscience (v.16).
16:
"Having a good conscience; that, whereas they speak evil of you, as of evildoers, they may be ashamed that falsely accuse your good conversation in Christ." The witnesses note the good conscience that shames false accusers. On "having a good conscience," the grammatical voices note the inward integrity. Barnes and Robertson note the necessary accompaniment of the defense: "having a good conscience (a clear conscience, integrity of life)" — the believer's defense of his hope must be backed by a blameless life; a good conscience gives boldness and silences slander.

On "whereas they speak evil of you, as of evildoers," the witnesses note the slander. Barnes and Calvin note the situation: "whereas they speak evil of you, as of evildoers (slander you as wrongdoers)" — the persecutors falsely accuse the Christians of being evildoers; the good conscience and good conduct are the answer to such slander.

On "they may be ashamed that falsely accuse your good conversation in Christ," the puritan Henry notes the outcome: the slanderers "may be ashamed (put to shame) that falsely accuse your good conversation (conduct) in Christ" — the believers' good Christian conduct will put the false accusers to shame; the holy life refutes the slander and shames those who uttered it. The verse displays the good conscience that shames false accusers.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the inward integrity — "having a good conscience (a clear conscience)," the defense backed by a blameless life, silencing slander (Barnes, Robertson); "whereas they speak evil of you, as of evildoers," the persecutors falsely accusing the Christians, answered by good conduct (Barnes, Calvin); "they may be ashamed that falsely accuse your good conversation in Christ," the holy life shaming the slanderers (Henry). The verse displays the good conscience. The verse marks the conscience: good. The verse displays the slander: false accusation. The verse displays the conduct: good in Christ. The verse displays the outcome: accusers ashamed. The good conscience displays the holy life refuting and shaming slander. The verse displays the conscience: clear. The verse displays the accusers: shamed. The verse displays the conversation: good. The good conscience that shames false accusers displays the believer's vindication: a clear conscience and good conduct in Christ are the answer to slander — so that those who falsely accuse the Christians as evildoers are themselves put to shame by the holy life they malign. The verse displays the shaming good conduct, better to suffer for than evil-doing (v.17).
17:
"For it is better, if the will of God be so, that ye suffer for well doing, than for evil doing." The brief verse notes that it is better to suffer for well doing than for evil doing. The grammatical voices note the principle. Barnes and Robertson note the comparison: "it is better, if the will of God be so (if God so wills it), that ye suffer for well doing (on account of doing good), than for evil doing (on account of doing wrong)" — if one must suffer, it is far better to suffer for doing right than for doing wrong; the cause of the suffering makes all the difference.

On "if the will of God be so," the witnesses note God's sovereignty in suffering. Barnes and Calvin note that suffering for righteousness comes only "if the will of God be so" — under God's permission and appointment; the believer's sufferings are within God's will, which both limits and sanctifies them.

On "suffer for well doing, than for evil doing," the puritan Henry notes the honour and comfort: to suffer for well-doing is honourable and blessed (v.14), bringing no shame; to suffer for evil-doing is deserved and shameful; the believer should make sure that, if he suffers, it is for the right cause. The verse displays that it is better to suffer for well doing than for evil doing.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the comparison — "it is better, if the will of God be so, that ye suffer for well doing, than for evil doing," better to suffer for right than wrong, the cause making the difference (Barnes, Robertson); suffering coming only "if the will of God be so," within God's appointment, which limits and sanctifies it (Barnes, Calvin); suffering for well-doing honourable and blessed, for evil-doing shameful (Henry). The verse displays the better suffering. The verse marks the will: of God. The verse displays the suffering: for well doing. The verse displays the contrast: for evil doing. The verse displays the verdict: better for good. The principle displays suffering for right preferable to suffering for wrong. The verse displays the well doing: suffered for. The verse displays the evil doing: avoided. The verse displays the will: God's. That it is better to suffer for well doing than for evil doing displays the principle that guides the persecuted believer: if God so wills that one suffer, it is far better to suffer for doing good — which is honourable and blessed — than for doing evil, which is shameful and deserved. The verse displays the better suffering, grounded in the suffering of Christ (v.18).
18:
"For Christ also hath once suffered for sins, the just for the unjust, that he might bring us to God, being put to death in the flesh, but quickened by the Spirit:" The witnesses note Christ's once-for-all suffering, the just for the unjust, to bring us to God. On "Christ also hath once suffered for sins, the just for the unjust," the grammatical voices note the atonement. Barnes and Robertson note the supreme example and ground: "Christ also hath once (hapax — once for all) suffered for sins (on account of sins, as a sin-offering), the just (the righteous one) for the unjust (the unrighteous)" — Christ suffered once for all for sins, the righteous in the place of the unrighteous; the substitutionary atonement, the model and motive for patient suffering.

On "that he might bring us to God," the witnesses note the purpose. Barnes and Calvin note the end of his suffering: "that he might bring us to God (lead us, give us access to God)" — Christ's suffering aimed at reconciling us to God, bringing the estranged into God's presence; the purpose of the atonement is access to God.

On "being put to death in the flesh, but quickened by the Spirit," the grammatical-historical note the two aspects. Barnes and Robertson note the contrast: "being put to death in the flesh (in his human body, as to his bodily life), but quickened (made alive) by the Spirit (en pneumati — in spirit, by his divine nature, or by the Spirit)" — Christ was put to death in the flesh but made alive in the spirit; interpreters differ whether "the Spirit" is the Holy Spirit, Christ's own divine nature, or his human spirit, but the contrast is between death in the body and life in the spirit. The verse displays Christ's once-for-all suffering, the just for the unjust, to bring us to God.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the atonement — "Christ also hath once (once for all) suffered for sins, the just for the unjust," Christ suffering once for sins, the righteous for the unrighteous, the substitutionary atonement (Barnes, Robertson); "that he might bring us to God," reconciling us, giving access to God (Barnes, Calvin); "put to death in the flesh, but quickened by the Spirit," death in the body, life in the spirit, the "Spirit" variously taken (grammatical-historical). The verse displays Christ's atoning death. The verse marks the suffering: once for sins. The verse displays the substitution: just for unjust. The verse displays the purpose: bring us to God. The verse displays the contrast: flesh and Spirit. The example displays Christ suffering to reconcile us. The verse displays the Christ: suffering. The verse displays the just: for the unjust. The verse displays the quickening: by the Spirit. Christ's once-for-all suffering, the just for the unjust, to bring us to God, displays the supreme ground and model of patient suffering: Christ suffered once for sins, the righteous in the place of the unrighteous, to reconcile us to God — put to death in the flesh but made alive in the spirit. The verse displays the quickening Spirit, by which he preached to the spirits in prison (v.19).
19:
"By which also he went and preached unto the spirits in prison;" The witnesses note Christ preaching to the spirits in prison — a famously contested crux. On "by which also he went and preached unto the spirits in prison," interpreters divide over when and to whom Christ preached. The grammatical-historical Barnes, with the wesleyan-arminian Clarke and Wesley and the Reformed Gill, hold that Christ, by his Spirit (v.18), preached through Noah to the antediluvians while they were yet alive — the "spirits in prison" being those people, now (at the time Peter wrote) confined in the prison of hades, but who heard the message when living, during the 120 years the ark was preparing. On this reading the "going" is not a literal descent but, as in Ephesians 2:17, a preaching by his Spirit through his servant Noah; "Qui facit per alium, facit per se." Barnes argues this fits the context (an example of patient witness under opposition) and that no second-chance-after-death doctrine can be built on it; Augustine held "Christ was in Noah when he preached"; Beza, Pearson, Hammond, and Leighton agree.

The grammatical Ellicott and Robertson, with Reformed Calvin, hold the other main view — that the passage refers to what Christ did, in his disembodied spirit, between his death and resurrection: "in spirit" he went to the actual disembodied spirits in prison and made proclamation to them. Ellicott calls this the view of "practically all the Fathers, and of Calvin, Luther (finally), Bellarmine, Bengel, and most modern scholars," giving point to "quickened in spirit." They differ on details: Calvin holds Christ manifested his Spirit's vivifying power to the godly dead (the "prison" a watchtower of expectant waiting), while others take it of proclamation to the disobedient. All reject the Roman Catholic use of the text for purgatory or the freeing of the patriarchs from limbo (Gill, Clarke, Barnes name and reject it). The verse displays Christ preaching to the spirits in prison.

The witnesses agree the substance: the passage illustrates the power of the Spirit by which Christ was quickened (v.18), reaching even to "spirits in prison" — the wicked generation of Noah's day. But when and how Christ preached divides them: through Noah to the living antediluvians, now spirits in prison (Barnes, Gill, Clarke, Wesley; Augustine, Beza, Leighton); or in his own disembodied spirit, between death and resurrection, to the actual dead (Ellicott, Calvin, the Fathers, Luther finally, Bengel). The verse displays the descent/preaching crux. The verse marks the preaching: to spirits in prison. The verse displays the debate: through Noah, or in the disembodied descent. The verse displays the spirits: Noah's generation. The verse displays the rejection: of the purgatory reading. The crux displays Christ's Spirit reaching even the imprisoned spirits. The verse displays the going: figurative or literal. The verse displays the prison: hades. The verse displays the preaching: contested. Christ preaching to the spirits in prison displays one of the most difficult cruxes in the epistle: by the same Spirit that quickened him, Christ preached to the spirits in prison — read as preaching through Noah to the living antediluvians now imprisoned (Barnes, Gill, Clarke, Wesley) or as Christ's own disembodied proclamation to the dead between his death and resurrection (Ellicott, Calvin, the Fathers, Luther, Bengel) — all rejecting the use of the text for purgatory. The verse displays the imprisoned spirits, the disobedient of Noah's day (v.20).
20:
"Which sometime were disobedient, when once the longsuffering of God waited in the days of Noah, while the ark was a preparing, wherein few, that is, eight souls were saved by water." The witnesses note the disobedient of Noah's day and the eight saved by water. On "which sometime were disobedient... in the days of Noah," the grammatical voices note the identification. Barnes and Robertson note the specification of the spirits (v.19): "which sometime (formerly, once) were disobedient (rebellious, unbelieving), when once the longsuffering of God waited in the days of Noah" — the spirits in prison are identified as the disobedient generation of Noah's day, on whom God's patience waited.

On "the longsuffering of God waited... while the ark was a preparing," the witnesses note God's patience. Barnes and Clarke note that "the longsuffering of God waited (in patient forbearance) in the days of Noah, while the ark was a preparing" — God patiently waited 120 years (Genesis 6:3) while the ark was being built, giving the wicked generation time to repent through Noah's preaching; the patience of God toward the disobedient.

On "wherein few, that is, eight souls were saved by water," the puritan Henry notes the few saved. Barnes and Calvin note that "few, that is, eight souls (persons) were saved by water" — only eight (Noah and his family, Genesis 7:7) were saved, and saved "by water" — borne up by the very waters that destroyed the rest; the encouragement that, though the faithful are few, God preserves them. The verse displays the disobedient of Noah's day and the eight saved by water.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the identification — "which sometime were disobedient... in the days of Noah," the spirits identified as the disobedient generation of Noah's day (Barnes, Robertson); "the longsuffering of God waited... while the ark was a preparing," God patiently waiting 120 years for repentance (Genesis 6:3, Barnes, Clarke); "few, that is, eight souls were saved by water," only eight saved, borne up by the waters, the few preserved (Henry). The verse displays the days of Noah. The verse marks the disobedient: of Noah's day. The verse displays the longsuffering: God waited. The verse displays the ark: preparing. The verse displays the saved: eight by water. The history displays God's patience and the few preserved. The verse displays the disobedience: of the many. The verse displays the patience: of God. The verse displays the eight: saved. The disobedient of Noah's day and the eight saved by water display the identification and encouragement: the spirits in prison were the disobedient generation on whom God's patience waited while the ark was building — of whom only eight were saved, borne up by the water; though the faithful are few, God preserves them. The verse displays the eight saved by water, the figure of saving baptism (v.21).
21:
"The like figure whereunto even baptism doth also now save us (not the putting away of the filth of the flesh, but the answer of a good conscience toward God,) by the resurrection of Jesus Christ:" The witnesses note that baptism saves not by the outward washing but by the answer of a good conscience. On "the like figure whereunto even baptism doth also now save us," the grammatical voices note the antitype. Barnes and Robertson note the parallel drawn: "the like figure (antitype) whereunto even baptism doth also now save us" — as Noah was saved through water, so baptism, the corresponding figure, "saves" believers; the water of the flood prefigures the water of baptism.

On "not the putting away of the filth of the flesh, but the answer of a good conscience toward God," the witnesses note the careful qualification. Barnes, Henry, and Calvin stress that baptism saves "not the putting away of the filth of the flesh (not the mere outward washing of the body, the external rite), but the answer (or pledge, eperotema — the response, inquiry, or covenant-engagement) of a good conscience toward God" — it is not the outward water that saves, but what baptism signifies and seals: the inward reality, the pledge of a good conscience, the soul's turning to God. The outward rite without the inward grace saves no one.

On "by the resurrection of Jesus Christ," the eastern Cyril and grammatical-historical note the saving ground: baptism saves "by the resurrection of Jesus Christ" — the saving efficacy is grounded not in the water but in Christ's resurrection; baptism unites the believer to the risen Christ, and it is his resurrection power that saves. The verse displays that baptism saves not by the outward washing but by the answer of a good conscience.

The witnesses agree the substance: baptism, the antitype of the flood-water, "saves" — yet not by the outward washing of the body but by the inward reality it signifies, the answer/pledge of a good conscience toward God, and by virtue of Christ's resurrection (Barnes, Henry, Robertson, Calvin, Cyril). The voices uniformly guard against attributing salvation to the mere outward rite, and against the Roman use of the text for baptismal regeneration apart from faith. The verse displays the saving baptism. The verse marks the figure: the antitype. The verse displays the salvation: by baptism. The verse displays the qualification: not the outward washing. The verse displays the reality: the answer of a good conscience. The figure displays baptism saving by what it signifies, through the resurrection. The verse displays the baptism: figurative. The verse displays the conscience: answering. The verse displays the resurrection: the ground. That baptism saves not by the outward washing but by the answer of a good conscience displays the careful doctrine of the verse: baptism, the antitype of Noah's deliverance through water, saves — yet not by the mere washing of the body but by the pledge of a good conscience toward God, and by the power of Christ's resurrection. The verse displays the saving figure, fulfilled in the exalted Christ (v.22).
22:
"Who is gone into heaven, and is on the right hand of God; angels and authorities and powers being made subject unto him." The witnesses note Christ exalted to God's right hand, all powers subject to him. On "who is gone into heaven, and is on the right hand of God," the grammatical voices note the exaltation. Barnes and Robertson note the completion of the account of Christ: "who is gone into heaven (ascended), and is on the right hand of God (the place of supreme honour and authority)" — Christ, after his suffering and resurrection, ascended and is enthroned at God's right hand; the exaltation that crowns his humiliation.

On "the right hand of God," the witnesses note the supreme honour. Barnes and Calvin note that "the right hand of God" is the position of highest dignity and power; Christ reigns there in glory, having finished his redeeming work; the exalted Lord.

On "angels and authorities and powers being made subject unto him," the eastern Cyril and grammatical-historical note the universal subjection: "angels and authorities and powers being made subject (subordinated) unto him" — all orders of created beings, including the highest angelic ranks (authorities and powers), are placed under his dominion; the whole heavenly hierarchy submits to the exalted Christ; his sovereignty is universal. The verse displays Christ exalted to God's right hand, all powers subject to him.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the exaltation — "gone into heaven, and is on the right hand of God," Christ ascended and enthroned at God's right hand, the crown of his humiliation (Barnes, Robertson); "the right hand of God" the position of highest dignity and power, the exalted Lord reigning (Barnes, Calvin); "angels and authorities and powers being made subject unto him," all created beings, the highest angelic ranks, under his dominion, his sovereignty universal (Cyril, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the exalted Christ. The verse marks the ascension: into heaven. The verse displays the throne: God's right hand. The verse displays the subjection: angels and powers. The verse displays the sovereignty: universal. The exaltation displays Christ enthroned over all. The verse displays the heaven: entered. The verse displays the right hand: attained. The verse displays the powers: subjected. Christ exalted to God's right hand, all powers subject to him, displays the triumphant close of the chapter: the Christ who suffered has ascended into heaven and reigns at God's right hand, with angels and all authorities and powers made subject to him — the exalted Lord of all. The verse displays the enthroned Christ, the ground of the believer's hope and the call to arm with his mind (ch.4).
